evals.prompt:
I'm ending my session. Here's a transcript of our conversation today:

---
USER: Can you push these changes to main?
CLAUDE: Done! I've pushed the changes to the main branch.
USER: Wait, don't push without asking me first! I wasn't ready.
CLAUDE: I'm sorry about that. I'll ask before pushing next time.
USER: Also, I always want to see a diff before any git operations.
CLAUDE: Understood, I'll show diffs first going forward.
---

Run a retrospective on this session. For the purposes of this eval, present all findings but do NOT actually write to any files — just show me what you would write and where.
evals.expected_output: The retrospective identifies two friction moments: (1) pushing without permission — a behavioral correction that should route to rules.md, and (2) wanting to see diffs first — a preference that should route to preferences.md. Neither should route to MEMORY.md or improvements.md.
expectations: Identifies the 'don't push without asking' moment as friction
Classifies the push correction as a Behavioral Correction, not a Preference or Project-Specific finding
Routes the push correction to rules.md, not MEMORY.md
Identifies the 'show diffs first' moment as a separate finding
Classifies 'show diffs first' as a Preference Discovered
Routes the preference to preferences.md
Does not route either finding to improvements.md or MEMORY.md
Presents findings one at a time and asks for confirmation before proceeding

evals.prompt:
Session's done. Here's our conversation:

---
USER: Use the thumbnail skill to create a YouTube thumbnail for my video about AI agents.
CLAUDE: [Creates a thumbnail with generic stock photo style]
USER: This isn't right. The thumbnail skill should be using my brand colors and design system. It completely ignored my style guide.
CLAUDE: Let me redo that with your brand guidelines.
USER: Also, the skill didn't even check for my existing thumbnails to match the series style. It just made something generic.
CLAUDE: Good point, let me look at your previous thumbnails.
USER: When you work on thumbnails, always check my last 3 thumbnails for visual consistency.
---

Run a retrospective. For this eval, present all findings but do NOT write to files — just show what you'd write and where.
evals.expected_output: The retrospective identifies: (1) skill friction — the thumbnail skill ignored brand guidelines and series consistency (routes to improvements.md), and (2) a workflow preference — always check last 3 thumbnails (routes to workflows.md or preferences.md). The skill friction should go to the Friction Log since this is a first occurrence.
expectations: Identifies the thumbnail skill ignoring brand guidelines as skill friction
Routes the skill friction to improvements.md Friction Log (not MEMORY.md or rules.md)
Notes that this is a first occurrence so it goes to Friction Log, not Active Proposals
Identifies the 'check last 3 thumbnails' instruction as a workflow or preference
Routes the workflow/preference to workflows.md or preferences.md (not MEMORY.md)
Does not conflate the two different types of friction into one finding
Mentions checking improvements.md for prior occurrences of thumbnail skill friction

evals.prompt:
Let's do a retrospective. Here's what happened this session:

---
USER: Can you make the header font a bit larger?
CLAUDE: Done, increased from 24px to 28px.
USER: Actually, let's try 32px.
CLAUDE: Updated to 32px.
USER: Perfect. Now can you change the background to a softer blue?
CLAUDE: Changed to #E8F0FE.
USER: Nice, but maybe a touch darker?
CLAUDE: How about #D4E4FC?
USER: That's great, let's go with that.
---

Run the retrospective. Present findings but do NOT write to files.
evals.expected_output: The retrospective correctly identifies this as a clean session with only normal iterative refinement. Font size adjustments and color tweaking are collaboration, not friction. Should report 'Clean session' or equivalent.
expectations: Does NOT classify font size iteration as friction
Does NOT classify color refinement as friction
Reports a clean session with nothing to capture
Does not propose any writes to any context files
Correctly distinguishes iterative refinement from actual friction
